title: Smooth velocity shuttling for suppressing valley excitations in disordered Si/SiGe quantum dots
authors: Ryo Nagai, Takashi Takemoto, Hiroyuki Mizuno
abstract: Coherent electron shuttling is a key requirement for realizing scalable silicon quantum computing architectures. However, in silicon qubits, the existence of nearly degenerate conduction-band valleys poses a significant challenge because non-adiabatic transitions to excited valley states cause spin dephasing via spin-valley mixing. In this paper, we propose a smooth velocity shuttling protocol to suppress these valley excitations. By mapping the time-domain design of the shuttling velocity profile onto the design problem of window functions in signal processing, we establish an analytical and intuitive design guideline that does not require computationally expensive numerical optimization. We demonstrate that the high-frequency sidelobes of the shuttling velocity spectrum can be effectively suppressed by applying a frequency-modulated gate voltage based on the Tukey window. Through numerical simulations incorporating realistic spatial randomness of the valley landscape, we show that the proposed smooth velocity control significantly reduces the average spin infidelity in the moderate-to-low disorder regime ($|Δ_0|/σ_Δ\simeq \mathcal{O}(1)$). Furthermore, we clarify that in devices designed with a large deterministic valley coupling $|Δ_0|$, combining it with this smoothing technique improves robustness against valley disorder. Our results underscore that this simple, control-level velocity shaping provides a robust pathway toward high-fidelity spin transport in large-scale silicon quantum processors.
summary: This paper introduces a novel, analytically derived method—smooth velocity shuttling—to suppress detrimental valley excitations in silicon quantum dots. By shaping the gate voltage using frequency modulation, the authors suppress high-frequency components in the shuttling velocity spectrum. This technique significantly improves spin coherence and fidelity, offering a robust pathway for large-scale silicon quantum computing architectures.
main_problem: The primary challenge is suppressing non-adiabatic valley excitations during electron shuttling in silicon quantum dots, as these transitions cause spin dephasing and limit qubit fidelity.
main_result: The authors propose a smooth velocity shuttling protocol, mathematically equivalent to window function design, which significantly reduces spin infidelity in the moderate-to-low disorder regime.
method: They derive an analytical guideline by mapping the time-domain velocity profile design onto signal processing window function theory, implementing this via frequency-modulated gate voltages.
model_or_system: The system is modeled using Si/SiGe quantum dots hosting spin qubits, analyzed via an effective Hamiltonian that includes valley and spin degrees of freedom. The dynamics are governed by the time-dependent shuttling velocity $v(t)$.
key_observables: Average spin infidelity, spin purity $P_s(t)$, and the probability of valley excitation $p_v$.
important_parameters: Disorder strength ratio $|\Delta_0|/\sigma_\Delta$, the smoothness parameter $ eta$, and the deterministic valley coupling $|\Delta_0|$.
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the perturbation approximation for valley transitions and assumes the applicability of the effective mass approximation for the potential landscape.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the comparison of velocity waveforms (conventional vs. smooth/Tukey-window based) and show the time evolution of spin impurity and infidelity, demonstrating fidelity maintenance with smooth control.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem (valley excitation during shuttling), derives the connection to signal processing window functions, proposes the FM gate voltage implementation, and validates the method through numerical simulations showing fidelity improvement.
why_interesting: This work provides a concrete, control-level engineering solution for mitigating decoherence sources (valley mixing) in solid-state qubits, which is highly relevant to building scalable quantum processors.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Shuttling velocity profiles (left) and spectra (right). The black curve corresponds to the time-dependence of the conventional shuttling velocity control (β = 0), and the light- blue and blue curves correspond to that of our proposed shut- tling controls with β = 0.5 and β = 1, respectively.

FIG. 2. Gate-voltage waveforms for implementing the pro- posed shuttling method (smooth velocity shuttling). The gate- voltage waveforms (12) can be generated by frequency modu- lation of the conventional sine waveforms (10) by signal s(t). The time dependence of the waveforms are shown in the top, middle, and bottom panels corresponding to β = 0, 0.5, and 1, respectively.

FIG. 3. Schematic diagram of the SiGe/Si/SiGe quantum dot shuttling device used in the numerical simulations. Gate electrodes are arranged at intervals of L along the shuttling direction (x-axis), and a frequency-modulated (FM) voltage Vm(t) with a period of N = 4 is applied. Rectangular gates for confining electrons in the y-direction are placed on both sides of the shuttling path, and a constant DC voltage VCG is applied. Electrons are confined within the quantum dots formed by these electrodes and transported through the Si well.

FIG. 4. Numerical calculation results of the smooth velocity shuttling protocol. From top to bottom, the changes of the signal wave s(t), the applied gate voltage Vn(t), the shuttling velocity v(t), and the quantum dot size ax(t) against the normalized time t/T are shown. It can be confirmed that the acceleration during transport is modulated by the velocity shaping parameter β, and the high-frequency components are suppressed.

FIG. 5. Examples of the valley coupling landscape ∆(x) along the shuttling path. Random spatial variations of the valley coupling are shown for various disorder strengths characterized by the ratio |∆0|/σ∆. A smaller ratio corresponds to a stronger influence of disorder induced by alloy fluctuations.
